takeaway: Motion graphic design is "a subset of graphic design which combines design with motion graphics and video production"; effective motion work draws on both design principles and animation principles.

Per NN/g, "animations can be leveraged for usability: as clues about what is currently happening with the system, as signifiers for how UI elements will behave, and as easily understandable spatial metaphors" — feedback, state changes, and orientation.

NN/g recommends UX animations of roughly 100–500ms (most 100–400ms): ~100ms for simple feedback, 200–300ms for modals, up to 400ms for large movements; "At 500ms, animations start to feel like a real drag." Use ease-out for entering elements, ease-in for exiting.

Material's standard (ease-in-out) curve has elements "quickly accelerate and slowly decelerate"; most mobile transitions are ~300ms (enter 225ms, exit 195ms), and anything over 400ms "may feel too slow." Movement should be fast enough not to wait, slow enough to understand.

An easing function "describes the rate at which a value changes" over an animation's duration; common ones are linear, ease, ease-in, ease-out, ease-in-out, and custom cubic-bezier() — linear feels mechanical, eased motion feels natural.

Google Design's Jonas Naimark frames Material motion as adjustable: "You can adjust a transition's duration, easing, and motion path to express the style of your app," with four transition patterns ranging from simple-functional to dramatic-emphasized.

Per NN/g, "Microinteractions convey system status, support error prevention, and communicate brand" — small feedback moments (toggles, loaders, button states) that turn a good experience into a great one.

Dan Saffer's Microinteractions defines them through a structure of "triggers, rules, feedback, loops, and modes" — tiny details that, done thoughtfully, differentiate a product; the discipline is doing much with little.

The Interaction Design Foundation maps Disney's principles to UI: anticipation (hover states signaling interactivity), easing (natural rather than robotic motion), and arcs (curved paths) make interfaces feel more intuitive and alive.

Val Head (author of Designing Interface Animation) argues Disney's classic principles remain relevant: "The 12 principles break down how to communicate with animation and how to control what it conveys to viewers" — easing choices shape perception much as typography does.

Disney animators Ollie Johnston and Frank Thomas introduced the 12 principles in their 1981 book The Illusion of Life; key ones include squash and stretch, anticipation (which "prepare[s] the audience for an action"), and slow in and slow out (the basis of easing).

Animation aids usability when it reinforces UI relationships or clarifies state changes in the user's focus, but harms it when gratuitous and repeated — users reported "this was nice the first time, but now it's getting annoying."

The prefers-reduced-motion media feature detects when a user wants "to minimize the amount of non-essential motion"; such animations "can trigger discomfort for those with vestibular motion disorders" — scaling or panning large objects are common triggers.

WCAG Success Criterion 2.3.3 requires that "motion animation triggered by interaction can be disabled, unless the animation is essential" — protecting users with vestibular disorders from non-essential motion that causes nausea or dizziness.

CSS-Tricks recommends using @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) to detect the OS setting and then reduce motion thoughtfully — leaning on fades and color changes rather than eliminating all movement, since "animation isn't unnecessary."
